coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as multifunctional electrolyte additives toward multicolor and low self-discharge electrochromic energy storage devices

Hu, Shengliang
Li, Chen
coredata.dc:description: Electrochromic energy storage devices (EESDs), integrating energy storage and electrochromism into one cell that can change appearance based on their working states, are presently attracting considerable attention in a wide range of applications. EESDs with long-term stability, multicolor display and slow self-discharge rate are highly desired to broaden their practical applications. However, considering the complexity of integrating various components and multi-layer configurations, obtaining high-performance EESDs with exceptionally aesthetic versatility and excellent anti-self-discharge function via simple approaches is still a big challenge. Herein, a novel concept of EESD with multicolor electrochromic displays and low self-discharge rate was successfully designed and fabricated via employing carbon dots (CDs) as electrolyte additives. The prototype EESD could not only present vivid colors in 2D regional space but also exhibit remarkable electrochromic and electrochemical performances, such as fast switching responses (4.3/5.6 s for the coloration/bleaching processes), high coloration efficiency (∼134.8 cm2 C − 1), high volumetric power/energy density (364 mW cm−3/34.7 mWh cm−3 and 7420 mW cm−3/17.4 mWh cm−3), along with outstanding cycling stability. Furthermore, the as-fabricated EESD can also demonstrate a reversible and steady electrochromic and electrochemical behavior under harsh temperatures between -25 °C and 65 °C, presenting enormous potential for consumers to monitor the working status of electronics in full-temperature conditions. Encouragingly, the resultant EESD exhibits slow self-discharge rate (such as 10.2 mVh−1at -25 °C, 19.4 mVh−1at 20 °C and 26.5 mVh−1at 65 °C), which are comparable with some previous researches that based on electrolyte anti-self-discharge modification. It is envisioned that this work may provide a feasible strategy to develop multifunctional EESDs for broad applications in military camouflage and intelligent electronics.

(c) Optical transmittance for the CDs-based electrolytes at the region of 300∼900 nm. (d) Ionic conductivities of the electrolytes with different mass fraction of CDs. (e) Comparisons of the transmittance and ionic conductivity of the developed electrolytes and some previously reported electrolytes.

(d) CIE color coordinates of the EESD (PB//WO3) at various colored states. (e) Digital images of the EESD (PB//WO3) realized 16 colors display.

(e) Self-discharge profiles under different initial voltages at 65 °C. (f) Loss voltage as a function of initial voltage at different temperatures. (g) Comparison of self-discharge rate with previously reported works. (h) Schematic illustration of the self-discharge mechanism.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as multifunctional electrolyte additives toward multicolor and low self-discharge electrochromic energy storage devices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrochromic energy storage devices (EESDs), integrating energy storage and electrochromism into one cell that can change appearance based on their working states, are presently attracting considerable attention in a wide range of applications. EESDs with long-term stability, multicolor display and slow self-discharge rate are highly desired to broaden their practical applications. However, considering the complexity of integrating various components and multi-layer configurations, obtaining high-performance EESDs with exceptionally aesthetic versatility and excellent anti-self-discharge function via simple approaches is still a big challenge. Herein, a novel concept of EESD with multicolor electrochromic displays and low self-discharge rate was successfully designed and fabricated via employing carbon dots (CDs) as electrolyte additives. The prototype EESD could not only present vivid colors in 2D regional space but also exhibit remarkable electrochromic and electrochemical performances, such as fast switching responses (4.3/5.6 s for the coloration/bleaching processes), high coloration efficiency (∼134.8 cm2 C − 1), high volumetric power/energy density (364 mW cm−3/34.7 mWh cm−3 and 7420 mW cm−3/17.4 mWh cm−3), along with outstanding cycling stability. Furthermore, the as-fabricated EESD can also demonstrate a reversible and steady electrochromic and electrochemical behavior under harsh temperatures between -25 °C and 65 °C, presenting enormous potential for consumers to monitor the working status of electronics in full-temperature conditions. Encouragingly, the resultant EESD exhibits slow self-discharge rate (such as 10.2 mVh−1at -25 °C, 19.4 mVh−1at 20 °C and 26.5 mVh−1at 65 °C), which are comparable with some previous researches that based on electrolyte anti-self-discharge modification. It is envisioned that this work may provide a feasible strategy to develop multifunctional EESDs for broad applications in military camouflage and intelligent electronics.

Image, graphical abstract
A novel and smart type of EESD with exceptionally aesthetic versatility and excellent anti-self-discharge function was successfully fabricated via utilizing CDs as the multifunctional electrolyte additives. Importantly, the multicolor EESD not only possesses high energy/power density, but also the working states could be monitored by chromatic transition. Significantly, the EESDs could also well maintain a slow self-discharge rate and their original electrochemical characteristics under various environmental temperatures.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochromic devices (ECDs) are some devices that present reversible changes in the electrochemical and optical performances (color, transmittance, absorption or reflectance) under various applied voltages [1–3]. Electrochromic energy storage devices (EESDs) that can visually indicate the working status via real-time color changes have attracted significant scientific and technological interests in many fields due to their exceptional optical modulation functions, such as smart windows, antiglare rearview mirrors, energy storage and adaptive camouflage [4–6]. However, developing all-solid-state EESDs with anti-self-discharge and multicolor display behavior is still a big challenge, especially those based on inorganic electrochromic materials. Generally, EESDs exhibit similar device construction to traditional electrochromic devices (ECDs), mainly include the transparent conductors, electrochromic positive/negative materials and ion electrolyte layers [7]. The electrochromic color types of most inorganic electrochromic positive/negative materials are relatively monotonous, such as tungsten oxide (WO3) in previous studies can only present a 1-fold color modulation from transparent to blue [8,9]. In this regard, fabricating complex and multicolored EESDs with pleasing visual aesthetics is not feasible, since materials that can simultaneously present outstanding electrochromic and electrochemical performances are restricted, diminishing the richness of design possibilities.

To date, extensive research has been launched into constructing multicolor EESDs based on WO3 electrochromic materials by element doping, designing Fabry-Pérot interferometer structure and incorporating the plasmon resonance phenomenon [6,10–13]. Nevertheless, such specific fabrication process or complicated structure is not only time-consuming and expensive but also causes inevitable interface issues or even sacrifices the transparency of the whole devices. For instance, Dahlin et al. constructed a Fabry-Pérot nanocavity-based colorful electrochromic display, where WO3 is sandwiched between a semitransparent Au film and a Pt mirror with nanopores fabricated by a colloidal lithography technique, enhancing both the chromaticity and brightness. However, the complex process of producing nanoholes, as well as the use of liquid electrolytes and precious metals, may restrict the scale-up, performance and wide application of these devices [14]. Moreover, the self-discharge (SDC) process of multicolor EESDs in extreme environments is usually ignored in works as mentioned above. SDC process is a spontaneous decrease of open voltage from a high energy state (fully charged) to a lower free energy state of energy storage devices, leading to low charging efficiency and loss of stored energy [15,16]. The fast SDC rate of EESDs is another crucial challenge for their practical applications, especially in a harsh environment. Various strategies have been developed recently to suppress the self-discharge of energy storage devices, such as modifying electrode materials, adding additives in electrolytes and designing novel separators [17–21]. Introducing functional additives into electrolytes seems to be the most convenient and efficient strategy. Therefore, the rational design and preparation of novel multifunctional electrolyte additives to mitigate the SDC behavior shows a promising potential to construct structural color-enhanced and low self-discharge EESDs.

Carbon dots (CDs), a new type of fluorescent carbon material with unique structures and excellent photoluminescence (PL) properties, have attracted tremendous attention in electrolytes and electrodes for energy storage devices [22–24]. CDs are generally composed of carbon cores and abundant amorphous surfaces functional groups, which could promote more electrolyte ions (such as Li+) adsorbed onto the active electrode, ameliorate the electric field distribution and improve reaction kinetics of electrolyte ions [25]. Moreover, CDs can be facilely synthesized on a large scale from simple precursors and then dispersed homogeneously in various electrolytes. Specifically, CDs can also easily achieve color-tunable emission in the visible region because of their unique PL performance [26,27]. Inspired by these advanced merits of CDs, we envisioned that functional CDs could be a promising electrolyte additive to suppress the rapid SDC process and present multicolored patterns of EESDs. So far, some previous works have confirmed that CDs as electrolyte additives are able to suppress the growth of dendrites, reduce side reactions and optimize electrolyte/electrode interfaces, which availably improve the overall performance of energy storage devices (Such as lithium-, sodium- and zinc-ion batteries) [28–30]. Additionally, our previous work has demonstrated that CDs could play positive roles in designing multicolor ECDs [31]. Therefore, combining the advantages of CDs and inorganic electrochromic materials to fabricate an all-solid-state EESD is expected to result in acceptable visual aesthetics and lower SDC rate, especially in harsh environments.

Herein, we demonstrated a novel concept of all-solid-state EESD with multicolor electrochromic displays and low self-discharge rate via employing CDs as electrolyte additives. As a proof of concept, the prototype EESD was constructed through sandwiching the gel electrolyte including CDs additives between the WO3 electrochromic negative electrode and the Prussian blue (PB) electrochromic positive electrode. Through the above design, the obtained EESDs could present various fantastic patterns and vivid colors in 2D regional space. Moreover, the resultant EESDs are sensitively changeable according to their working states and simultaneously possess superior electrochromic and electrochemical performances, such as fast switching responses (4.3/5.6 s for the coloration/bleaching processes), high coloration efficiency (∼134.8 cm2 C−1), high power/energy density (364 mW cm−3/34.7 mWh cm−3 and 7420 mW cm−3/17.4 mWh cm−3), along with excellent electrochemical cycle-life (around 98.4 % capacitance retention after 1000 long-term cycles even at 65 °C). Significantly, the prototype EESD exhibited slow self-discharge rate (such as 10.2 mVh−1 at −25 °C, 19.4 mVh−1 at 20 °C and 26.5 mVh−1 at 65 °C), which are comparable and even lower than that many pervious works. Additionally, the detailed self-discharge mechanisms of multicolor EESDs were comprehensively unraveled at different working temperatures. Considering that the multicolor and anti-self-discharge EESD is still in the infancy stage, the prototype EESD with large charge capacity, multicolor display and slow self-discharge rate provides a new insight for multicolor electrochromic displays and multifunctional energy storage devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of single electrode composites and carbon dots (CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Prussian blue (PB) films were electrodeposited on the ITO substrate through the constant current density electrodeposition method using the electrochemical workstation CS2350. The electrochemical deposition was carried out in a three-electrode system with Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode and platinum worked as the counter electrode. The electrodeposition solution was composed of 0.01 mol/L of anhydrous ferric chloride (FeCl3), 0.1 mol/L of potassium chloride (KCl), and 0.01 mol/L of potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]). The ITO was electrodeposited in the above solution at −0.04 mA cm−2 for 300 s to obtain the ITO/PB electrode. As a result, a uniform blue film was observed to form on the ITO/PB electrode. WO3 films were deposited onto the ITO glass substrates via a simple magnetron sputtering method (Fig. S1, Supporting Information). Before the deposition, the W-target was pre-sputtered for 10 min with a shutter to remove contaminants from the surface of the target. The distance from the target to the substrate was fixed at 15 cm. The sputtering power and working pressure were set as 150 W and 2.0 Pa, respectively. Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared via the reaction of coal pitch powder with a mixed solution of H2O2 and formic acid without any external heating. Typically, 3 mL of H2O2 (30 wt%) and 30 mL of formic acid were mixed in a 200 mL open flask under stirring, and then 200 mg powder of coal pitch was added to the above solution. Subsequently, the resultant slurry was stirred for 18 h under the conditions. After removing unreacted power by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 30 min, the suspension containing CDs was obtained. The excess H2O2 and formic acid were removed by solution boiling and then washed with deionized water several times. The final products were dried in the oven at 60 °C for 5 h to obtain the dark brown CDs powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembly of multicolor electrochromic energy storage device (EESD)
ce:section.ce:para: A typical EESD was assembled using the PB/ITO positive electrode and the WO3/ITO negative electrode, and 1 M LiClO4/Propylene carbonate (PC)/polyurethane acrylate (PUA) with CDs additives aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte. A double-faced adhesive with 1 mm thickness was employed as the spacer between the positive (PB) and negative (WO3) electrodes. The tape and UV curing agent sealed all the components and assembled them into a chamber-like EESD with a 2 × 2 cm2 work area, and the electrolyte solution was injected into the inner space of the whole device. At last, the as-fabricated EESD was placed under an ultraviolet (UV) lamp for 15 min to solidify the electrolyte.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Nano SEM450) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20) were employed to measure the surface morphologies and microstructures of as-obtained samples. The composition, structure and chemical bonding configurations of the as-fabricated samples were characterized via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS SUPRA), Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR, Bruker Vertex 70) and Raman spectroscopy (LabRam HR-800, 514 nm) techniques.

The electrochemical characterizations (such as cyclic voltammetry (CV), chronoamperometry (CA), chronopotentiometry (CP), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS)) were performed on an electrochemical workstation (CS2350H, Wuhan CorrTest) using a three-electrode system for the single electrode and a two-electrode configuration for the whole device. The total transmittance spectra and in-situ optical transmittance were evaluated by a homemade UV–Vis spectroscopy (PUXI, TU1901, China). Further details for the in-situ measurement process are displayed in Fig. S2 (Supporting Information). Long-term cycling stability data of samples were recorded using a LAND testing system (CT3001A).

The carbon dots (CDs) were fabricated through selectively oxidizing and etching coal tar pitch with HCOOH and H2O2 ratio solution at room temperature and pressure (Figs. 1a and S3, Supporting Information). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image reveals that as-fabricated CDs exhibit good dispersion with uniform sizes of ∼3.8 nm (Fig. 1b). Moreover, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image presents the prominent lattice fringe. As shown in Fig. 1c, the lattice spacing of the as-obtained CDs is about 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (001) crystal planes of graphite. Furthermore, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was also employed to demonstrate the surface functional groups of CDs. As shown in Fig. 1d, a broad absorption peak at 3420 cm−1 is characteristically stretching vibration of the hydroxyl group. The absorption bands at around 2928, 1750 and 1242 cm−1 are attributed to the C-H, O-C=O and C-O vibration from CDs, while the weak C=C stretching is also observed at 1565 cm−1 [32].

In addition, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were further conducted to analyze the element composition of the CDs. The survey scan spectrum confirms the presence of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen elements in the resultant CDs (Fig. S4a, Supporting Information). Obviously, as displayed in Fig. 1e, the high-resolution spectra of C 1 s can be deconvoluted into four peaks, which are assigned to C=C (284.4 eV), C-O (286.1 eV) and C-OH (288.2 eV), respectively [30,33]. Moreover, the O 1 s spectrum in Fig. 1f can also be deconvoluted into more components, suggesting various O species during CDs. Clearly, three types of peaks at 531, 531.9 and 533.5 eV were fitted, corresponding to O-C=O, C-O-C and COOH, respectively [34]. Additionally, Raman spectra present typically graphene-like sharp peaks, suggesting a large amount of amorphous structures within CDs (Fig. S4b, Supporting Information) [35]. The above characterization results show that the CDs with functionalized hydroxyl and carbonyl groups have been successfully fabricated (Fig. 1h). For some gel polymer electrolytes, there exist a limited number of noncontinuous amorphous chains that can dissociate electrolyte salts and transfer Li-ions, the long and flexural ion transmission path results in low ionic conductivity. In contrast, the addition of well-dispersed CDs results in strong Lewis acid-base interactions among CDs, electrolyte salts and solvents [28]. These interactions not only increase the free volume and segmental motion by increasing the amorphicity of polymer matrix but also enhance the dissociation of electrolyte salts and adsorption of cations, all of which facilitate Li-ions transport through the creation of low-energy ion conduction pathways along the CDs and polymer matrix interface [34,36]. The CDs contain rich surface functional groups served as electrolyte additives, which are expected to facilitate the preferential adsorption and adjust Li ions transmission, resulting in high-performance electrochromic energy storage devices (EESDs).

Importantly, as shown in Fig. 2a (top), the CDs possess outstanding dispersity in propylene carbonate (PC) organic solvents, which is suitable for constructing high-performance gel polymer electrolytes. Moreover, Fig. 2a (bottom) shows the optical photographs of CDs dispersed in the PC solvent under UV light. Obviously, the solution with uniformly distributed CDs presents an obvious fluorescence feature (Fig. S5, Supporting Information). The optical performance of PC solvent with various amounts of CDs was analyzed and presented in Fig. 2b. With the increased mass of the added CDs, the transmittance of the PC solvent gradually decreases. Interestingly, there is a redshift phenomenon and an optical modulation range of the PC solvent, which makes CDs have great application potential in multicolor EESDs. The electrolyte layer, which provides Li+ ions and is also regarded as a separator, plays an essential role in EESDs. A kind of electrolyte layer based on PC-LiClO4 and polyurethane acrylate (PUA) was fabricated at CDs mass ratio of 0.1 %. Benefiting from the homogeneous structure, the electrolyte layer possesses outstanding optical transparency with a thickness of 2 mm (inset of Fig. 2c). Obviously, the electrolyte layer presented an average transmittance of over 85 % at the visible region of 300∼900 nm, which is sufficiently adequate for fabricating high-performance EESDs. Additionally, the CDs-based electrolyte also exhibits a wide electrochemical voltage window up to 3.8 V (Fig. S6, Supporting Information).

Ionic conductivity is another core feature of the electrolyte layer in applications and usually determines the switch time of EESDs [37]. The ionic conductivity of the electrolyte layer with various CDs mass ratios is shown in Figs. 2d and S7a (Supporting Information). At room temperature, the ionic conductivity considerably increases and then decreases with the increase of the concentration of CDs, suggesting that the conductivity of the electrolyte layer can be effectively regulated by adding CDs additives. Notably, the resultant electrolyte layer constructed at CDs mass ratio of 0.25 % could obtain a relatively high ionic conductivity of 0.16 mS cm−1, which is a suitable value for electrolytes of EESDs. Furthermore, the ionic conductivities of the as-obtained electrolyte as a function of the working temperature are also shown in Figure S7b (Supporting Information). Additionally, a comprehensive comparison of the transmittance and ionic conductivity of previous literature and this work is presented in Fig. 2e [37–47]. As can be seen, our CDs-based electrolyte could provide relatively high ionic conductivity and transmittance, outperforms most recent state-of-the-art works and is particularly suitable for applications in EESDs.

The process of the fabrication and the configuration of the obtained EESDs are shown in Fig. 3 a. According to the above method, four kinds of EESDs (PB//WO3, MnO2//WO3, PANI//WO3 and V2O5//WO3) with same electrolyte (PC+LiClO4+PUA) were successfully constructed. The EESD of these four systems all present good spectrum and color regulation abilities (Fig. 3b, c). When the applied voltage changed between −1.5 V and +1.5 V, the optical modulation at 630 nm of these four devices can reach 64.8 % (PB//WO3), 55.2 % (MnO2//WO3), 24.1 % (PANI//WO3) and 41.5 % (V2O5//WO3), respectively. As can be seen from Fig. 3c, although EESDs based on inorganic electrochromic materials can achieve a certain degree of color regulation, such as PB//WO3, MnO2//WO3, and V2O5//WO3 systems, they still suffered from problems of low color saturation and poor color-tuning versatility. Benefiting from the synergistic effect of organic and inorganic electrochromic materials, the color hues of the EESD based on PANI//WO3 could be broadened, but its color tenability is still distributed as a linear or curved segment in the International Commission on Illumination (CIE) color space. To address the above matters, a novel EESD was fabricated via sandwiching the CDs-based gel electrolyte between the WO3 and PB electrochromic electrodes. Previous research works have demonstrated that WO3 and PB exhibit outstanding electrochromic and electrochemical performance, and have been widely employed as negative/positive electrodes to construct high-performance electrochromic devices [3,48–51]. Their electrochromic and electrochemical behavior are also measured and shown in Figs. S8–S10 (Supporting Information).

Through the elaborated design, the obtained EESD could be synthetically tuneable to produce various color states, resulting in a near full-color palette that has long been expected of EESDs (Fig. 3d). Significantly, the EESD presented rich and real-time color changes during the electrochemical process. Fig. 3e demonstrates the corresponding optical images of the CDs-based EESDs under different representative color states. Owning to the color overlay effect of the CDs and electrochromic materials, the prototype EESD forms a two-dimensional chrominance coordinate diagram distribution in the CIE color space, resulting in a widened color palette. As a transmission type color display device, the thickness of the whole device and the intensity of natural light will determine the brightness of colors. Some colors, such as dark blue and dark brown, appeared might be attributed to the relatively thick total thickness of the resultant EESD. These rich colors were also quantitatively measured by the transmittance spectrum at the region of 300–900 nm. Fig. S11a (Supporting Information) demonstrates the optical transmittance spectra of the obtained EESD as the CDs in the electrolyte remain at a specified mass (e.g., 0.1 wt%, 0.25 wt%, 0.5 wt% and 1.0 wt%) and the whole device tunes its colors at different applied voltages (−1.5∼+1.5 V). As for the V2O5//WO3 system, multicolor EESD can also be obtained by adding CDs into the electrolyte, further confirming above strategy is feasible (Fig. S11b and Video S1, Supporting Information).

To date, many EESDs only presented their vivid color changes at room temperature. As well known, the temperature varies across the country and even around the world, developing all-climate and multicolor EESD will promote its more comprehensive application, especially under extreme environmental conditions [52–54]. Encouragingly, the prototype EESD (electrolyte including 0.1 wt% CDs) also exhibits reversible and excellent electrochromic behaviors under different working temperatures from −25 °C to 65 °C. Fig. 4a shows the transmittance spectrum of the EESD at colored and bleached states under various working temperatures. As can be seen, when working at −25 °C, 20 °C and 65 °C, the maximum optical modulation of the obtained device at 630 nm could reach 37.6 %, 48.9 %, and 55.2 %, respectively. Moreover, the coloration switching time (tc) is determined to be 4.3 s, and the bleaching time (tb) is 5.6 s when the resultant EESD works at 65 °C (when at −25 °C, tc=6.4 s and tb=10.6 s, based on Fig. 4b). These values are comparable to those devices based on traditional gel electrolytes [55,56]. Furthermore, owing to the incorporation of the CDs-based electrolyte, the prototype EESD at various temperatures still possesses a relatively high coloration efficiency of approximately 83.6 cm2 C−1 (−25 °C), 112.5 cm2 C−1(20 °C), 134.8 cm2 C−1(65 °C), which are comparable with some reported all-solid-state electrochromic devices based on inorganic electrochromic electrodes (Fig. 4c) [57–59]. As demonstrated by the transmittance of various working states, the coloration efficiency and response time, some changes in electrochromic properties can be found for the fabricated EESD, which might be ascribed to the different mobility of Li-ions during the electrochemical process under various working temperatures.

Significantly, the resultant EESD also possessed superior cycling adaptability and stability at an all-climate temperature range from −25 °C to 65 °C. Figs. 4d, e and S12 (Supporting Information) show the in-situ transmittance change of the obtained EESD during several hundred electrochemical cycles at various temperatures. Notably, the electrochromic performance of the whole device is still affected by the previous temperature. When the working temperature dropped from 20 °C to −25 °C, the optical modulation of our fabricated EESD was reduced by nearly 20 %. Encouragingly, after experiencing some electrochemical cycles of the EESD at low temperatures (such as −25 °C), the transmittance modulation could almost recover to the initial value at room temperature. With the increasing temperature, the optical modulation gradually increased and obtained the maximum optical modulation of 53.6 % at 65 °C. After cycling at 65 °C, a slight decrease of the optical modulation appeared when the temperature again back to 20 °C. The degradation caused by high temperatures (such as 65 °C) is often irreversible rather than the excellent recoverability of the device performance at low temperatures (such as −25 °C). Additionally, as shown in Figs. 4f and S13 (Supporting Information), again confirm that the prototype EESD possesses impressive temperature adaptability. Obviously, about 97.4 %, 95.7 % and 84.5 % of the initial transmittance modulation can be sustained under −25 °C, 20 °C and 65 °C working temperature even after 500 cycles, indicating outstanding cycling stability of the CDs-based EESDs against temperature variations.

To further demonstrate the thermal durability and electrochemical performance under different environment temperatures, Fig. 5 a-b show the CV and GCD profiles of the prototype EESD under various working temperatures. As can be seen from Fig. 5a, the profile at 50 mV s−1 displays two couples of well-resolved redox peaks at around 0.36/0.23 V (peaks A/A′) and 1.18/1.21 V (peaks B/B′), which corresponds to reversible Li-ions abstraction/intercalation within the active electrode materials. Clearly, these redox peaks were observed to be symmetric, implying excellent reversibility of the corresponding electrochemical reactions. Moreover, although the integral area of the CV curves changes significantly when the temperature increases from −25 °C to 65 °C, they always maintain a similar shape. The area enclosed of the CV profile enlarged with temperature, suggesting that the increasing capacitance of the resultant EESDs is attributed to the rapid migration of Li-ions in the electrolyte at high temperatures. Remarkably, as the working temperature increases, the EESD exhibited a longer charge/discharge time, which matches well with the CV profile as the capacitance increases with the temperature (Fig. 5b). To further demonstrate the rate performance of the as-fabricated EESD at different operating temperatures, typical CV profiles were recorded at different scan rates varying from 5 to 200 mV s−1 and charge/discharge measurements were performed at different current densities from 0.1 to 1 mA cm−2 (Figs. S14, S15, Supporting Information). Furthermore, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) diagram presents a small semicircle at the high-frequency range and a sloping line at the low-frequency region, suggesting a stable charge transport and a low charge transfer resistance. Meanwhile, the obtained EESD almost exhibits the same internal resistance (Rs) values when under various temperatures, which might be owe to the rapid Li-ions migration of the CDs-based electrolyte at different working temperatures. However, with an increase in the temperature, the charge-transfer resistance (Rct) values of the EESDs gradually decreased, further confirming the outstanding electrochemical behavior with rapid ion diffusion rate at elevated temperatures. Importantly, the lower interface and charge-transfer resistance could promote Li-ions diffusion to the electrode interface, resulting in increased charge storage and capacitance of the resultant EESD.

Furthermore, the volumetric and area capacitances of the obtained EESD as a function of scan rates under various operating temperatures are compared in Figs. 5d and S16 (Supporting Information). Clearly, at the same scanning rate, the capacity gradually increases when raised the operating temperatures. With the increasing sweep rates, the volumetric capacitance becomes smaller and smaller, owing to inadequate Li-ions intercalation into the electrolyte materials at fast scan rates. Additionally, as the sweep rates increased from 5 to 200 mV s−1, the prototype EESD can still maintain 61.3 % of its initial capacitance at 65 °C, which is higher than 46.1 % at −25 °C, suggesting superior rate performance and high reversibility. To further evaluate the practical application potential of the fabricated CDs-based EESD, Fig. 5e shows a Ragone plot of the gravimetric power/energy density for the EESD compared to previously reported energy storage devices. Remarkably, the resultant EESD could provide high volumetric power/energy density (364 mW cm−3/34.7 mWh cm−3 and 7420 mW cm−3/17.4 mWh cm−3), which is even comparable to some commercially available energy storage devices (such as near 2∼3 orders of magnitude higher than commercial supercapacitors). Moreover, even when the power density is increased to 7420 mW cm−3, the obtained EESD can remain at 50.4 % of its energy density, indicating superior power capabilities. Furthermore, an energy density of 125 mWh cm−3 at low temperatures (such as −25 °C) was much larger than those of some previously reported EESDs. Benefiting from the synergy of the components of the whole (including positive/negative electrodes, electrolyte and current collector), this stack volumetric energy density of 34.7 mWh cm−3 is comparable and even superior to many previously reported energy storage devices, such as Zn//MgVO [60], WO3//MnO2 [50], LTO//AG [61], f-SCs [62] and so on.

Apart from the remarkable electrochemical energy storage performance, the resultant EESD also exhibited excellent temperature tolerance (Fig. 5f, g). Obviously, during the continuous heating process at a temperature range of −25 °C∼65 °C, the volumetric capacitance of the device increased as increasing operation temperature due to the rapid Li-ion diffusion kinetics. When the working temperature further increases and reaches 80 °C, the volumetric capacitance decreases with increasing temperature, which might be caused by the decomposition of CDs-based polymer electrolytes and thus slightly decreases the energy capacity (Figs. 5f and S17, Supporting Information) [63]. Based on the above results, the optimal operating temperature range of the device can be determined to be between −25 °C and 65 °C. Notably, although experiencing a sharp change within a wide temperature range, there was almost no obvious loss of volumetric capacitance at each stage (Fig. 5 g). When the working temperature again back to 20 °C after repeat heating-cooling-heating 2500 cycles at the operating temperature range from −25 °C to 65 °C, the resultant EESD still could maintain 95.6 % of its initial volumetric capacitance, which further confirming that the prototype EESDs exhibit outstanding temperature-resistive stability within a wide temperature range. Furthermore, the as-fabricated could successfully light up a red LED for as long as 20 min, even after a long-term cycle at various temperatures (inset in Figs. 5 g and S18, Supporting Information), providing a reliable for the practical application under an extreme environment. In particular, the resultant EESD could deliver 93.2 % capacitance retention even after 1000 consecutive CV cycles at 65 °C, indicating no obvious deterioration when working at such a wide temperature range (Fig. 5h). As demonstrated and analyzed above, our newly-developed EESD based on CDs electrolytes possessed stable and reversible electrochemical performance under various temperatures (Fig. 5i), comparable with most previously reported temperature adaptive energy storage devices and EESDs [52,60,62,64–69].

Self-discharge behavior is another essential metric for EESDs in practical applications [70,71]. Herein, the as-obtained EESDs based on CDs electrolytes were subjected to self-discharge performance measurements (Fig. S19a, Supporting Information). Firstly, the resultant EESD was charged to a specific voltage with a constant current load, and then held the terminal voltage for 5 min to dismiss the charge inhomogeneity. Lastly, the open-circuit voltage was measured and recorded. Significantly, the resultant EESS could maintain about 96.3 % of its initial capacity even after 180 min voltage holding time, suggesting that the voltage holding exhibits negligible effects on the electrochemical performance of the whole device (Fig. S19b, Supporting Information). Obviously, adding CDs additives into the electrolyte could significantly impact the self-discharge process for the resultant EESD compared to that in the devices without CDs (Fig. 6a ). As can be seen, the EESD with CDs-based electrolytes could maintain the open circuit voltage for a longer time than the same device without CDs. Moreover, with the increasing mass of the added CDs, the suppressive effect on the self-discharge behavior is increasingly obvious.

To verify the intrinsic driving force and better understand the self-discharge process, Figs. 6b and S20 (Supporting Information) show the open voltage curves as a function of log(t) and t1/2, respectively. As can be seen, the self-discharge curve first presents an initial plateau and then follows a linear drop, which agrees well with the characteristic curves of both the charge redistribution and activation-controlled mechanism [17,72]. Generally, the length of the plateau could provide some critical mechanistic information. As for a charge redistribution mechanism, the plateau length depends on the charge redistribution time constant, and a more extended plateau indicates an increased charge redistribution time. Similarly, the plateau length is inversely related to the initial self-discharge voltage for an activation-controlled mechanism. As can be seen from Fig. 6b, the length of the initial plateau gradually increases with the increase of CDs content. When the EESD was charged to 1.6 V, there are two stages of diffusion-controlled processes can be observed. The resultant EESD presented a rapid voltage drop, related to the charge redistribution after removing applied constant voltage, while another drop stage of voltage, diffusion-controlled self-discharge due to reorganization of ions inside the electrode. Taking the above analysis results into consideration, the self-discharge process of the prototype EESD is mainly determined by a mixed mechanism containing both diffusion-controlled and activation-controlled at a particular voltage of 1.6 V. Encouraging, the self-discharge process could be effectively improved through adding CDs additives into the electrolyte, leading to the resultant EESD with an anti-self-discharge characteristic.

Generally, self-discharge is a rate process based on well-known kinetic effects associated with activation energy [73]. Thus, the self-discharge rates of the as-obtained EESD under various temperatures are generally accelerated or retarded. To further demonstrate the effect of working temperature on self-discharge rate, Fig. 6d shows the self-discharge profiles of the obtained EESD at −25 °C, 20 °C and 65 °C. Clearly, the as-obtained EESD possessed the slowest self-discharge rate at a low temperature (such as −25 °C). In contrast, the self-discharge rate will be accelerated at high temperatures owing to an improved electrochemical activity of Li-ions. Importantly, even after 10 h with no load at 65 °C, the open voltage of the as-fabricated EESD only drops by around 15.8 % and the voltage can be maintained above 1.35 V, comparable with previously reported similar devices [74,75].

To further reveal the self-discharge mechanism of the prototype EESD at a wide temperature range, Fig. 6e and Figure S21 (Supporting Information) show the voltage decay curves as a function of different open voltages between 0.8 and 1.6 V at −25 °C, 20 °C and 65 °C, respectively. As expected, the diffusion-controlled and activation-controlled mixed model applied at room temperature is still effective in the wide temperature range of the as-fabricated EESD. According to the previous literature results, if the open voltage of the resultant EESD decreases to half of the initial value, it will lose 75 % of its initial capacity, leading to inefficient energy efficiency and fatal damage to the electrochemical and electrochromic performance [76,77]. As can be seen, when charged to 0.8, 1.0, 1.2, 1.4 and 1.6 V, the as-obtained EESDs could remain 91.7 %, 88.1 %, 85.6 %, 84.5 % and 84.2 % of the initial voltage even after 10 h self-discharge process, respectively. These values are all over 75 %, suggesting the voltage decay was substantially restricted through adding CDs additives into the electrolyte of the resultant EESD. Meanwhile, Fig. 6f shows the voltage loss of the prototype EESD after 10 h self-discharge processes at different operating temperatures and various initial voltages. Clearly, when at the same temperature condition, the lower initial open voltage of the EESD presents a smaller voltage loss than the higher initial voltage. Importantly, even working at 65 °C and the initial voltage reaches 1.6 V, our EESD could still maintain 87.6 % of its initial open voltage value over a 10-hour self-discharge process. Moreover, the as-fabricated EESD also exhibited a remarkable optical memory effect under various working temperatures (Fig. S22a, Supporting Information). These above results confirm that the resultant EESD based on CDs possesses anti-self-discharge performance. To the best of our knowledge, this good anti-self-discharge performance is comparable and even superior to some energy storage devices with electrolytes modification via using surfactant additives, ionic liquids and liquid crystal (Figs. 6 g and S22b, Supporting Information) [20,78–84]. Obviously, benefiting from the synergistic effect of electrolyte/electrode, our as-fabricated EESDs exhibit a slow self-discharge rate even under harsh environments, enabling it to possess a broad application prospect and potential in smart electronic devices. Finally, the self-discharge regulation mechanism and schematic construction are demonstrated in Fig. 6h. Due to the abundant functional groups, the as-obtained CDs usually present weak electronegativity when added to the electrolyte. The negatively charged state of the CDs surface is certified by the zeta potential measurements (Fig. S23, Supporting Information). The negative charge (−26.4 mV) of CDs in propylene carbonate (PC) organic solvent indicates the high affinity toward electrode owing to abundant functional group (-COOH and -OH), which is in favor of repelling the approaching of anion. When the EESD is charged/colored, the CDs would be absorbed on the surface of the PB positive electrode, resulting in a surface cover layer. On the contrary, when removing the externally applied voltage, the internal electric field would drive the cations and the anions to go into the electrolyte. At the same time, the strong repulsive force and the surface cover layer (formed through CDs, Fig. S24, Supporting Information) can efficiently slow down the movement of ions, leading to reduced leakage current and suppressed self-discharge behavior of the fully charged EESD. Considering the above analysis, adding the CDs into the electrolyte could not only reduce charge transfer reactions at the electrode surface but also cause slower diffusion of ion at no load state, which are beneficial to the inhibition of the self-discharge rate.

In summary, a novel and smart type of EESD with exceptionally aesthetic versatility and excellent anti-self-discharge function was successfully fabricated via utilizing CDs as the multifunctional electrolyte additives. The assembly process and structure of the resultant EESD are straightforward. The obtained device can provide rich color tunability over a wide color gamut distribution through the color overlay effect, which is distinct from conventional ECDs based on inorganic materials. Moreover, benefiting from the synergetic effect of electrolyte and active electrode, the prototype EESD could deliver a high volumetric power/energy density (364 mW cm−3/34.7 mWh cm−3 and 7420 mW cm−3/17.4 mWh cm−3), and also possesses remarkable electrochemical cycling stability (around 98.4 % capacitance retention after 1000 long-term cycles even at 65 °C). Furthermore, the resultant EESD also exhibits a reversible and steady electrochromic and electrochemical performance even under harsh temperature conditions (such as from −25 °C to 65 °C), presenting enormous potential for consumers to monitor the working status of electronics in full-temperature conditions. Encouragingly, the prototype EESD exhibits slow self-discharge rates (such as 10.2 mVh−1 at −25 °C, 19.4 mVh−1 at 20 °C and 26.5 mVh−1 at 65 °C), which are comparable with some previous researches that based on electrolyte anti-self-discharge modification. Our exciting results may provide new avenues for developing intelligent and multifunctional EESDs that have long been desired for commercial innovative electronics.
